---
title: "LRP-vs-cash backtest — methodology"
subtitle: "How the per-cwt LRP advantage is computed, what it actually measures, and what it does not capture."
toc: true
toc-depth: 2
---

## What is being measured

The [LRP backtest page](../lrp-backtest.qmd) summarizes a single per-endorsement statistic: the dollar advantage, per cwt of cattle insured, of having bought LRP versus not having bought it. The chart aggregates this advantage by coverage-level percent and slices the corpus by state and year window.

This is a deliberately narrow question. The page's [About section](../lrp-backtest.qmd#about-this-page) flags what is and isn't covered; this methodology page expands those points formally.

## Data source: SOB participation, not the offered rate sheet

This page operates on RMA's Summary of Business (SOB) "Livestock and Dairy Participation" file — a record of LRP endorsements that producers actually purchased. Each row is one endorsement, with the producer's chosen coverage level, the producer-paid premium, and the indemnity (if any) that RMA paid out at end-date. We download the annual zip files from `pubfs-rma.fpac.usda.gov`; see [`pipelines/lrp/ingest.py`](https://github.com/lma-explorer/lma-explorer.github.io/blob/main/pipelines/lrp/ingest.py) for the pipeline.

This is a different data source from RMA's daily "offered rate sheet" — the prices RMA was offering on each business day to anyone, regardless of whether a purchase occurred. [Boyer & Griffith (2021)](https://www.choicesmagazine.org/choices-magazine/submitted-articles/livestock-risk-protection-payments-for-feeder-cattle-during-covid-19), the closest published LRP-Feeder Cattle retrospective, use the rate-sheet population (5,128,664 daily offerings in their final 2014–2020 sample after their filters).

The distinction matters in interpretation. SOB rows are real producer purchase decisions and therefore carry selection-effect signal: producers who bought LRP did so because of their perceived downside risk at the moment of purchase, not because RMA was offering a particular price (see [Selection effects](#selection-effects)). Rate-sheet rows are RMA's pricing for everyone and carry no purchase-decision information; they describe the menu of what was on offer, not what producers chose. Modest differences between this page's per-coverage-level means and Boyer & Griffith's published numbers should be expected from this data-source distinction (and from a few methodological choices documented elsewhere on this page), not attributed to error in either approach.

## The advantage formula

For each LRP endorsement in the [type-code subset](#type-code-subset) of the corpus, the advantage is:

$$
\text{LRP advantage}_{\text{per cwt}} = \frac{\text{indemnity\_amount} - \text{producer\_premium\_amount}}{\text{total\_weight\_cwt}}
$$

where the three columns are taken directly from the RMA Summary of Business as documented in [`pipelines/lrp/parse.py`](https://github.com/lma-explorer/lma-explorer.github.io/blob/main/pipelines/lrp/parse.py). The numerator is in dollars; the denominator is in hundredweight; the ratio is in dollars per cwt.

Sign convention:

- **Positive** ⇒ the producer-paid premium was less than the indemnity received. The producer would have been better off buying LRP than not buying it for this endorsement.
- **Zero** ⇒ break-even. (Mathematically narrow; in practice, exact zeros are rare.)
- **Negative** ⇒ the producer-paid premium exceeded the indemnity received. The producer would have been better off without LRP for this endorsement.

#### Why per cwt, and what it implies

The numerator of the formula (indemnity − premium) is in raw dollars, but raw-dollar advantage depends heavily on how many head an endorsement covered. A 10-head endorsement and a 1,000-head endorsement at the same coverage level can show a 100× difference in raw dollar advantage purely because of scale. Dividing by `total_weight_cwt` normalizes for that, so the resulting statistic is comparable across endorsements regardless of size. This is what makes a coverage-level comparison meaningful — it answers "did LRP at 95% pay better per pound of cattle covered than LRP at 80%?" rather than "did producers who chose 95% happen to insure more cattle than producers who chose 80%?"

A consequence: the per-cwt statistic is **invariant to the share of a producer's herd insured under any one endorsement**. A producer who insures 50% of their herd under one endorsement and a producer who insures 100% of their herd under another — both at the same coverage level, in the same year — produce different `total_weight_cwt`, different indemnity, and different premium values. But the ratio (indemnity − premium) / weight is the same in expectation, because each row scales linearly with the cattle weight under the policy. The per-cwt view isolates the LRP product's economic effect on the head it covered, not the producer's gross dollar exposure to the program. This is intentional: a chart organized by raw dollar advantage would conflate "LRP performed well" with "the producer insured a lot of cattle."

#### Equal-weight averaging across endorsements

When the chart aggregates across endorsements within a coverage-level bin, each endorsement contributes equally to the mean — one row, one observation. This is **decision-level weighting**: every producer's coverage-level choice is treated as one data point, regardless of the number of head behind it. An alternative — sum-of-numerators divided by sum-of-denominators (a head-weighted, or volume-weighted, average) — would weight larger endorsements more heavily, which answers a different question (the program's economic effect on cattle, not on producer decisions). The decision-level view is what producers and Extension peers most naturally read off the bar heights: the average per-cwt outcome across producer choices in that coverage band.

The page's chart shows the **arithmetic mean** of this statistic across all endorsements in the selected (state, year window) slice, grouped by coverage-level percent. The mean is sensitive to outliers; the corresponding median is computed by `pipelines/lrp/backtest.aggregate_by_coverage` and exposed in the underlying data — the chart shows mean rather than median because the mean is the unbiased estimator of the long-run average advantage that a representative producer would realize across many endorsements.

## What "advantage" measures

The formula computes the producer's **net LRP cash flow** per cwt: indemnity from RMA minus producer-paid premium, divided by share-adjusted hundredweight. This is mathematically unambiguous — every endorsement in the corpus carries its own indemnity and producer-premium amounts, and the per-cwt result is the producer's actual net financial impact from the LRP transaction itself. The same definition (indemnity minus producer premium, sometimes called "net payout" or "net price" in the literature) is used in published LRP-Feeder Cattle retrospectives such as [Burdine & Halich (2014)](https://www.jstor.org/stable/10.2307/jasfmra.2014.160) and [Boyer & Griffith (2021)](https://www.choicesmagazine.org/choices-magazine/submitted-articles/livestock-risk-protection-payments-for-feeder-cattle-during-covid-19).

Calling this "advantage" relative to not buying LRP requires one additional step: comparing the producer's outcome WITH LRP against their outcome WITHOUT LRP. The natural framing is two paths over the same period:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LRP path net} &= \text{cash\_at\_end} \times \text{cwt} + \text{indemnity} - \text{premium} \\
\text{No-LRP path net} &= \text{cash\_at\_end} \times \text{cwt} \\
\text{Difference} &= \text{LRP net} - \text{No-LRP net} = \text{indemnity} - \text{premium}
\end{aligned}
$$

The cash term drops out **only under the assumption that the producer's cash sale would have been identical in both paths** — same timing, same price, same channel, same hedging stance for the same physical animals. In practice that assumption is heroic. A producer who buys LRP may also change their sale timing, market channel, or hedging position relative to what they would have done without LRP. The selection-effect dimension of this — that producers who buy LRP do so because they expect a downturn — is acknowledged in the existing literature ([Burdine & Halich, 2014](https://www.jstor.org/stable/10.2307/jasfmra.2014.160)) and is discussed further in the [Selection effects](#selection-effects) subsection below. To the extent the producer's behavioral choices differ between the two paths, the formula does not measure the producer's full financial difference from buying LRP.

What the formula DOES measure cleanly is the LRP product's contribution to the producer's net cash position: RMA paid out X dollars in indemnity, the producer paid Y dollars in producer premium, and the per-cwt difference is what shows up on the chart. [Boyer & Griffith (2021)](https://www.choicesmagazine.org/choices-magazine/submitted-articles/livestock-risk-protection-payments-for-feeder-cattle-during-covid-19) frame their analogous statistic explicitly as "the average difference in indemnity and producer premiums" — the same per-cwt LRP cash flow we display, just temporally aggregated rather than coverage-binned. The number on our chart is correct for what it is. Whether the producer's all-in financial position was net better or worse with LRP also depends on second-order behavioral choices the formula does not see.

## What the formula does *not* capture

### Opportunity cost of capital

The producer pays the premium up front (at sale-effective date) and receives any indemnity later (at end-date). LRP feeder endorsement lengths are 13, 17, 21, 26, 30, 34, 39, 43, 47, or 52 weeks. At a 5 % annual real cost of capital, the discounting effect on a 26-week endorsement is roughly 2.5 % of the premium amount. The advantage formula treats indemnity and premium as time-equivalent; for a producer running tight cash flow, the practical advantage is slightly worse than the page reports.

This omission is small but consistent. A producer who wants to apply a discount factor can do so directly from the published producer-paid premium and indemnity columns; the corpus is fully reproducible.

### Basis-risk surprise

RMA's `actual_end_value` (the CME-futures-derived ending value used to determine indemnity) and the producer's cash market move together but not perfectly. There are scenarios where:

- The producer's *cash* dropped substantially over the endorsement period, but futures held above the coverage price → no indemnity, premium fully out of pocket. The producer might have *wanted* protection (because cash dropped) but the LRP product, by design, does not cover cash drops directly.
- Futures dropped substantially but the producer's cash held → an indemnity was paid even though the producer's local market didn't need it.

The advantage formula measures what the LRP product *did* (paid or didn't pay), not what the producer might have *wanted* given their cash market. A producer who is mostly worried about a cash-market crash specific to their auction or region needs to think about basis risk separately. The [Basis](../basis.qmd) page is the platform's view on Clovis-vs-CME-GF futures basis dynamics; the [Basis methodology](basis.qmd) page documents the formal distinction between strict basis and weight-class spreads.

### Variance reduction and the risk-management dimension

The advantage formula measures the LRP product's *cash-flow* impact: indemnity received minus producer-paid premium, normalized to dollars per cwt. It does not measure LRP's effectiveness as a *risk-management instrument* — its ability to reduce the variance of the producer's revenue stream, or to truncate the lower tail of their price-realization distribution. Those are different questions. A producer might rationally pay an actuarially negative-NPV premium for the variance-reduction benefit alone, the same way they pay for fire insurance not expecting to come out ahead in cash terms.

[Feuz (2009)](https://doi.org/10.22004/ag.econ.53046) provides the canonical comparison framework for this dimension, evaluating LRP against futures, options, and AGR-Lite for cow-calf producers in terms of risk-management effectiveness rather than cash-flow advantage. A producer assessing whether LRP is a good risk-management tool — as opposed to whether LRP paid out more than it cost on average — should consult that literature directly. This page does not attempt to replicate or summarize that comparison; the chart's per-cwt advantage is one input to a broader risk-management decision, not the whole question.

### Selection effects

The corpus contains every LRP endorsement that was actually sold. Producers who bought LRP did so because, at the moment of purchase, they expected a downturn in the cattle market or wanted to hedge a specific marketing decision (this self-selection is acknowledged in [Burdine & Halich, 2014](https://www.jstor.org/stable/10.2307/jasfmra.2014.160) — they note explicitly that LRP buyers' "perceived downside price risk" is the load-bearing variable in the purchase decision). These are not random samples of cattle-marketing situations.

The advantage formula treats every endorsement as an i.i.d. observation. The unconditional mean reported on the chart is therefore a *lower bound* on the conditional value to the producers who actually self-selected into the program with information about their own situation that the historical data doesn't capture. A producer with strong forward-looking views about price direction may well do better than the unconditional mean shown.

This is a feature of any historical backtest of an option-like product, not a flaw in the formula. It is also one reason our chart's analysis differs from offered-rate-sheet retrospectives like Boyer & Griffith (2021), which compute averages across all LRP contracts on offer rather than across actual purchases — the latter (our approach) carries selection information from real producer decisions, while the former does not.

## Regime changes within the data window

The data window (RY 2003 onward) spans two regulatory changes that materially affect what "the same coverage level" means or costs across years. The chart's "All available years" view aggregates across both changes silently; readers comparing slices that span these boundaries should know the underlying program was structured differently on either side.

### The 2020 subsidy regime change

The producer-paid share of LRP-Feeder Cattle premium has changed twice within our data window. Per [Boyer & Griffith (2021)](https://www.choicesmagazine.org/choices-magazine/submitted-articles/livestock-risk-protection-payments-for-feeder-cattle-during-covid-19), citing USDA RMA, the subsidy structure has been:

- **2003–2018:** 13% premium subsidy (producer pays 87% of total premium).
- **2019:** 20% subsidy (producer pays 80%).
- **2020 onward:** Tiered subsidy by coverage level — 35% for 95–100% coverage, 40% for 90–94.99%, 45% for 85–89.99%, 50% for 80–84.99%, 55% for 70–79.99%. Producer pays 45–65% of total premium depending on coverage choice.

Our pipeline ingests `producer_premium_amount` directly from each endorsement's RMA record, so each row correctly reflects the subsidy regime in effect when it was sold. The data is internally consistent.

**What this pooling does not show** is that pooling 2003–2018 endorsements (low subsidy, high producer cost) with 2025–2026 endorsements (tiered higher subsidy, lower producer cost) mixes producers who faced different premium economics. The same nominal coverage level was substantially more affordable to a 2025 producer than to a 2014 producer; the per-cwt advantage is correspondingly less negative on average in the post-2020 era for any given coverage level.

For analyses that should isolate one subsidy regime, use the **Year window** filter:

- "Latest" (current RY only) is always within a single regime.
- "Last 3 yrs" and "Last 5 yrs" are within the post-2020 tiered regime as of RY 2024 and beyond.
- "All available years" pools across all three regimes.

### The 2025 coverage-level menu regime change

The semantics of `coverage_level_pct` (the chart's x-axis variable) changed materially between RY 2024 and RY 2025.

**Pre-2025 (RY 2003 through 2024):** `coverage_level_pct` is a derived ratio — the producer's chosen coverage_price divided by the expected end value at sale. It is continuous. Our corpus contains hundreds of unique values per year in this period; only 0.2–3.3% of pre-2025 endorsements happen to land on what is now the 2026 fixed menu.

**2025 onward:** Per the [2026 LRP Insurance Standards Handbook (FCIC-20010 §22.B.3)](https://www.rma.usda.gov/policy-procedure/crop-underwriting-standards/livestock-risk-protection-handbook-2026), authorized coverage levels are a fixed 12-value menu: 75%, 80%, 85%, 87.5%, 90%, 92.5%, 95%, 96%, 97%, 98%, 99%, and 100%. Empirically, RY 2025–2026 endorsements in our corpus land 100% on this menu (12 unique values total).

The chart's 5-percentage-point range bins (70–75%, 75–80%, …, 95–100%, plus a 100% bar for the maximum-coverage choice available since 2025) are the right binning strategy for both regimes. **What this pooling does not show** is the meaning of a given bar:

- A pre-2025 "90–95%" bar pools producers whose chosen coverage_price happened to fall in [0.90, 0.95) of expected end value — a continuous distribution that naturally clusters near round percentages but is not a menu choice.
- A 2025+ "90–95%" bar pools producers who explicitly elected 90% or 92.5% from the menu — a finite set of identical decisions across the population.

Both are legitimate aggregations of "endorsements where the producer wanted approximately 90–95% coverage," but the underlying decision process is different. Readers comparing pre-2025 and 2025+ slices should know they are comparing two different decision regimes pooled into the same bin label.

## Sample size and confidence intervals

The chart's per-bin mean is supported by a vertical **95 % confidence interval** error bar and a sample-size label. Both reflect the underlying statistical reliability of the bar.

### 95 % CI computation

Each bar's confidence interval is computed as
$$
\bar{x} \pm 1.96 \cdot \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}
$$
where $\bar{x}$ is the bin's per-endorsement arithmetic mean, $s$ is the sample standard deviation of per-endorsement advantage within the bin, and $n$ is the number of endorsements. The factor 1.96 corresponds to a 95 % two-tailed normal interval.

When a bar's CI crosses the zero line, the bar's mean is **not statistically distinguishable from zero** at the 95 % level. Visually: a green bar whose lower error-bar end dips below zero, or a red bar whose upper error-bar end rises above zero, should be read as "directionally suggestive but not strong evidence" rather than "LRP paid more / cost more on average."

### Suppression of small-N bins

Coverage-level bins where the slice contains fewer than 30 endorsements are **suppressed** from the chart visual. A note below the chart reports how many bins were hidden for the current filter; the underlying data including suppressed bins is in the downloadable corpus.

The 30-endorsement threshold is the conventional rule-of-thumb minimum for the central-limit-theorem normality approximation underlying the 95 % CI calculation. Below that threshold, the CI bounds become unreliable estimates of the true population mean's interval; the per-bin mean itself becomes a noisy estimate.

For a state like Arizona — the platform's producer-reference state, which has fewer LRP endorsements per year than larger cattle states — this rule means several coverage-level bars routinely disappear from the chart. **This is intentional.** Showing a bar built on, say, two endorsements as visually equivalent to a bar built on 153 endorsements is the kind of visual error that ends a presentation. The honest position is: when the slice doesn't have enough data to support a coverage tier's bar, no bar is shown.

If the chart looks sparse for a particular state and year window, switching to a wider year window ("All available years") or comparing against "National (all states aggregated)" usually fills more bars.

### Descriptive vs inferential framing

The 95 % CI formula above assumes endorsements within a bin are independent draws from a stationary distribution. In practice they are not: endorsements cluster by year, market regime, and producer behavior. A 90–95 % bin in a cycle-trough year is dominated by indemnity-paid endorsements; the same bin in a cycle-peak year is dominated by premium-only endorsements. A more careful inferential treatment would correct for this clustering — for example, by computing year-clustered standard errors or by treating year as a random effect — and would typically widen the bands relative to the i.i.d. CI shown here.

The error bars on the chart are therefore best read as **descriptive uncertainty bands around each bin's historical mean**, not as inferential intervals on a forecasted future outcome. The break-even-line cross-check still has signal (a bin whose i.i.d. CI does not cross zero is at minimum directionally supported by its sample), but it should be read as *"the historical sample at this coverage level shows a mean clearly different from zero"* rather than as *"the next endorsement at this coverage level has a 95 % chance of falling within these bounds."*

This is a known limitation of the current implementation. A clustered-SE refactor is queued as `v2.lrp-backtest-clustered-uncertainty`.

### Why mean and not median

The chart shows the mean per coverage-level bin rather than the median. The per-endorsement advantage is highly variable — particularly in cattle-cycle-trough years (2009, 2016, 2020, parts of 2024), when indemnity payments were large, and in cycle-peak years, when premiums were paid but no indemnity arrived. Two coverage-level bins with the same mean can have very different distributions; the median, computed in [`pipelines/lrp/backtest.aggregate_by_coverage`](https://github.com/lma-explorer/lma-explorer.github.io/blob/main/pipelines/lrp/backtest.py), is typically smaller in absolute value than the mean for both signs.

The mean is the right summary statistic for a *long-run average* question (which is what the chart asks: "across the years and producers in this slice, what was the average per-cwt advantage?"). For a producer who would buy LRP only once or twice in a career, the median or a percentile-based summary may be more relevant; the underlying-data column for `median_advantage_per_cwt` supports that view.

### Indemnity probability vs. magnitude

The chart reports the *magnitude* of the LRP advantage in dollars per cwt — the size of the average payout (or net cost) per pound of cattle covered. A complementary statistic is the *probability* of an indemnity at all — the share of endorsements in a bin where `indemnity_amount > 0`. [Merritt et al. (2017)](https://doi.org/10.1017/aae.2016.44) studied this directly for LRP-Feeder Cattle, fitting probit models on the offered-rate-sheet population to estimate how indemnity probability varies with coverage level, contract length, and seasonal factors. They find that higher coverage levels and longer contract lengths both raise indemnity probability, conditional on the rate-sheet pricing.

Our chart's underlying data exposes the same probability statistic per coverage bin as `indemnified_share`, available in the downloadable corpus. We display mean magnitude on the chart visual because that is the dollar-impact question producers most directly ask, but the probability dimension is in the data for any reader who wants to look at it side by side with the magnitude bars.

## Type-code subset

The chart restricts to four RMA type codes — the same subset the descriptive [LRP page](../lrp.qmd) and its [methodology page](lrp.qmd) use:

| Code | Type name (modern) | Why included |
|---|---|---|
| **809** | Steers Weight 1 | Conventional non-Brahman, non-dairy, born |
| **810** | Steers Weight 2 | Conventional non-Brahman, non-dairy, born |
| **811** | Heifers Weight 1 | Conventional non-Brahman, non-dairy, born |
| **812** | Heifers Weight 2 | Conventional non-Brahman, non-dairy, born |

Excluded: Brahman (813/814), Dairy (815/816), Unborn variants (817/818/819), and the new Unborn Calves type 823 (which uses $/head pricing rather than $/cwt and would corrupt the per-cwt advantage formula). See the [LRP methodology page](lrp.qmd) for the full type-code-evolution discussion.

### Comparison with published retrospectives

[Boyer & Griffith (2021)](https://www.choicesmagazine.org/choices-magazine/submitted-articles/livestock-risk-protection-payments-for-feeder-cattle-during-covid-19) restrict their analysis to "feeder cattle … steers and heifers between 600 and 900 pounds" — a weight-range filter rather than a formal type-code restriction. Functionally, that filter implies the Weight 2 class (~700–899 lbs in current RMA pricing tables) and the heavier portion of Weight 1 (~600–699 lbs); it does not include lighter-weight calves. Our type-code subset {809, 810, 811, 812} is the corresponding RMA-coded universe — Steers W1+W2 (809/810) and Heifers W1+W2 (811/812) — and therefore covers a slightly broader weight range on the lighter end. For producers in larger cow-calf operations (the platform's reference state is Arizona) this difference matters in principle, but in our corpus the weight distribution of W1 endorsements skews toward the heavier W1 range, so practical overlap with Boyer & Griffith's population is high. Readers comparing per-coverage-level means against their published numbers should expect modest differences attributable to this and to the [SOB-vs-rate-sheet data-source distinction](#data-source-sob-participation-not-the-offered-rate-sheet).

## State and year-window filters

State filtering is applied directly on `state_abbr`. The chart's "National" option aggregates across all states.

### The `XX` / "All Other Counties" sentinel

RMA emits a sentinel four-tuple — `state_abbr="XX"`, `state_fips=99`, `county_fips=999`, `county_name="All Other Counties"` — in **16.22% of LRP-Feeder Cattle SOB rows** (34,129 of 210,375 rows in our 2003–2026 corpus). The sentinel is present in **every** reinsurance year, including the most recent (14.3% of RY 2026 rows), so it is not a pre-2021 schema-evolution artifact.

What RMA's documentation says about this sentinel: nothing we could find. The published LRP-SOB record layout (as of 2021-06-24) defines the four state/county fields generically without naming the `XX/99/999` sentinel; the M13 Handbook P99E FIPS State Code Exhibit lists codes 01–78 only; the SOB application Help page is UI-only. RMA Bulletin COM-16-001.1 ("Non-Disclosure Statements") concerns NDA paperwork for AIPs and contractors, not cell-suppression in published SOB data.

What the empirics say: `XX` rows are distinct observations, not duplicate aggregates of per-state rows. They are ~10–12% of per-state premium volume per year (not ~100% as a rollup would be) and have smaller mean per-endorsement characteristics (fewer head, smaller premium, smaller weight) than per-state rows. The most plausible interpretation, by analogy to USDA NASS's [documented "Combined Counties" practice](https://www.nass.usda.gov/Data_and_Statistics/County_Data_Files/Frequently_Asked_Questions/index.php) (NASS aggregates endorsements with "less than three reports for the item or one operation has more than 60 percent of the total" "to avoid disclosure of individual operations"), is that RMA aggregates endorsements that cannot be attributed to a specific state/county under federal non-disclosure rules. We cannot confirm this from RMA-authored documentation; if we eventually receive an authoritative RMA reply we will update this section.

**Pipeline policy on this page:** the chart's "National" option **includes** `XX` rows in the per-cwt advantage aggregation. The backtest is per-endorsement and never renders a state map, so XX endorsements are admissible — they are real LRP volume that just lacks a state attribution. Per-state filters (Arizona, Texas, etc.) necessarily exclude `XX` because we don't know which state the rows belong to. The descriptive [LRP explorer](../lrp.qmd) follows the same policy for its National headline numbers; see its [methodology page](lrp.qmd) for additional context.

### Year-window filter

Year-window filtering uses the same `apply_year_window` helper as the LRP page, with bounds computed relative to the corpus's most recent year (so "Last 5" stays current as new RMA Summary-of-Business years land). See [`pipelines/lrp/load.py`](https://github.com/lma-explorer/lma-explorer.github.io/blob/main/pipelines/lrp/load.py) for the bound-computation logic.

The chart's default "All available years" includes the full 2003-present span, but pre-2021 LRP-Feeder Cattle participation is sparse for most states — the [2018 Farm Bill subsidy expansion](https://www.ers.usda.gov/topics/farm-bill/2018-farm-bill/dairy-livestock) is the load-bearing volume driver, not state-coding maturation. Producers and Extension peers reading state-specific bars in earlier years should treat them with a small-sample caveat (the per-bin suppression rule below already handles this automatically).

## Coverage-level binning

The corpus stores `coverage_level_pct` as a float64 column. **It is not a discrete menu choice** — RMA does *not* require LRP-Feeder Cattle producers to select a coverage level from a fixed menu of percentages. Producers choose a specific `coverage_price` in dollars per cwt (subject to a 70%–100% floor-relative-to-`expected_end_value` band), and the corpus records `coverage_level_pct` as the derived ratio:

$$
\text{coverage\_level\_pct} = \frac{\text{coverage\_price}}{\text{expected\_end\_value}}
$$

Empirically, this means `coverage_level_pct` is **continuous, not discrete**. Approximately 42 % of TX endorsements (reinsurance years 2022–2026) land on round 5 % values like 0.7500, 0.8000, 0.8500, 0.9000, 0.9500 — suggesting many producers anchor on those round-number coverage prices. The remaining 58 % spread continuously across the 0.70–1.00 range, with values like 0.8807, 0.8936, 0.9063 each typically held by 1–3 endorsements.

The chart therefore **does not snap to a fixed menu** (which would erase real producer-choice signal) and does not bin at 1 % resolution (which produces noisy bars with tiny per-bar samples). Instead, it bins continuous coverage choices into **5-percentage-point ranges**:

- 70–75 % (i.e., 0.70 ≤ x < 0.75)
- 75–80 %
- 80–85 %
- 85–90 %
- 90–95 %
- 95–100 %
- ≥ 100 %

Bins are left-closed, right-open. Each bin's lower edge serves as its identifier in the underlying data structure; the chart's x-axis labels show the human-readable range. A producer reading "90–95 %" on the chart is reading the average of all endorsements whose `coverage_level_pct` falls in `[0.90, 0.95)` for the selected slice — a population that includes both the round 0.9000 anchors and the 0.9001, 0.9047, 0.9105, ... continuous selections.

This range-binning is methodologically defensible because:

1. **It does not erase real signal.** A producer who chose `coverage_price = $241.50` against an `expected_end_value` of $267.00 (yielding `coverage_level_pct = 0.9045`) made a real distinct choice from a producer who chose `coverage_price = $254.00` against the same `expected_end_value` (`coverage_level_pct = 0.9513`). Their premiums differ; their indemnities differ. Snapping both to "90 %" or "95 %" would mask that.
2. **It matches how producers think.** The coverage-level decision producers walk through with an Extension agent or insurance agent is "do I want to insure 80 %, 85 %, 90 % …" of the expected ending value. 5-percentage-point ranges align directly with that decision frame.
3. **It pools enough endorsements per bar for the mean to be stable.** With 7 bins across ~12,000 TX endorsements, each bar represents roughly 1,700 endorsements on average — enough that the mean is a useful statistic.
4. **It is robust to data evolution.** If RMA changes its coverage-price quoting practice in future years (finer or coarser increments), 5 % range bins continue to work without re-binning every prior year.

The implementation is in [`pipelines/lrp/backtest.aggregate_by_coverage`](https://github.com/lma-explorer/lma-explorer.github.io/blob/main/pipelines/lrp/backtest.py), using `pandas.cut` with edges `[0.70, 0.75, 0.80, 0.85, 0.90, 0.95, 1.00, ∞)`. Endorsements with `coverage_level_pct < 0.70` (rare; typically corrupt early-year rows) are dropped, as are rows with NaN coverage levels.

### Floor at 70% and the contrast with Boyer & Griffith

The chart includes endorsements across the full RMA-offered band of `coverage_level_pct ∈ [0.70, 1.00]`. [Boyer & Griffith (2021)](https://www.choicesmagazine.org/choices-magazine/submitted-articles/livestock-risk-protection-payments-for-feeder-cattle-during-covid-19), the closest published LRP-Feeder Cattle retrospective, exclude coverage levels under 85% from their analysis because in their offered-rate-sheet population (2014–2020) endorsements at <85% were less than 2% of the data and they wanted the analysis to reflect the modal coverage-level decision. We retain the 70–85% endorsements for two reasons:

1. **They are real producer choices.** In the SOB participation data this page operates on, endorsements at 70–85% coverage represent a deliberate trade-off (lower premium in exchange for a lower payout trigger). Excluding them would be an analytical choice, not a data-source constraint, and would suppress signal about how producers at the lower end of the coverage band actually fared. The corpus's purpose is descriptive: report what happened, including at the parts of the coverage band that some published analyses omit.
2. **The per-bin suppression rule already handles small-N transparently.** Bins below `MIN_BIN_N = 30` are visually hidden regardless of where they fall on the coverage axis. A 70–75% bin in a state with very few low-coverage endorsements simply disappears from the chart, with the suppression count noted in the inline disclosure line below the chart. The chart never displays an unreliable bar for the low-coverage range.

The 70% floor itself is the LRP-Feeder Cattle product's RMA-offered minimum, per the [2026 LRP Insurance Standards Handbook (FCIC-20010 §22.B.3)](https://www.rma.usda.gov/policy-procedure/crop-underwriting-standards/livestock-risk-protection-handbook-2026). Endorsements below 0.70 in our corpus are under 0.5% of rows and typically reflect early-year data-quality issues; we drop them along with NaN coverage levels.

## Reproducing the chart

The full pipeline is in two files:

- [`pipelines/lrp/backtest.py`](https://github.com/lma-explorer/lma-explorer.github.io/blob/main/pipelines/lrp/backtest.py) — `compute_advantage`, `backtest_subset`, `aggregate_by_coverage`, `aggregate_by_year`, and `sample_summary`.
- [`site/lrp-backtest.qmd`](https://github.com/lma-explorer/lma-explorer.github.io/blob/main/site/lrp-backtest.qmd) — the chart page itself, including the JS sync layer.

To reproduce the chart locally:

```python
import pandas as pd
from pipelines.lrp.load import load_corpus
from pipelines.lrp.backtest import aggregate_by_coverage

loaded = load_corpus(Path("data/processed"))
df = loaded.df

# AZ, all available years, coverage-level bar means
agg = aggregate_by_coverage(df, state_abbr="AZ", year_window="all")
print(agg[["coverage_level_pct", "n_endorsements", "mean_advantage_per_cwt"]])
```

## Future work

Tracked as candidate v2 items:

- **`v2.lrp-backtest-by-type`** — Drill down by type code (809/810/811/812 individually). Producers buying LRP for heavier feeders may see different advantage distributions than producers buying for lighter calves.
- **`v2.lrp-backtest-spread`** — Add an error bar or percentile band to each coverage-level bar to show the inter-quartile range of advantage at that coverage level. The mean alone hides a lot of variance.
- **`v2.lrp-backtest-cycle-overlay`** — Overlay the cattle cycle (e.g., USDA cow inventory year-over-year change) so a reader can see whether high-advantage years cluster in cycle troughs.
- **`v2.lrp-backtest-discount`** — Apply a discount factor for the time between premium-paid and indemnity-received. Small effect at typical endorsement lengths but worth surfacing for completeness.

## Code

- Chart page source: [`site/lrp-backtest.qmd`](https://github.com/lma-explorer/lma-explorer.github.io/blob/main/site/lrp-backtest.qmd)
- This methodology page's source: [`site/methodology/lrp-backtest.qmd`](https://github.com/lma-explorer/lma-explorer.github.io/blob/main/site/methodology/lrp-backtest.qmd)
- Backtest pipeline module: [`pipelines/lrp/backtest.py`](https://github.com/lma-explorer/lma-explorer.github.io/blob/main/pipelines/lrp/backtest.py)
- Underlying corpus parser: [`pipelines/lrp/parse.py`](https://github.com/lma-explorer/lma-explorer.github.io/blob/main/pipelines/lrp/parse.py)

## Contact

Spotted an error or a methodological disagreement? Open a [new issue](https://github.com/lma-explorer/lma-explorer.github.io/issues/new/choose) with the `methodology` label.
